description: GitLab CI/CD OIDC subjects against classic Azure federated-identity-credential (FIC) subjects (azure-fic-exact). Third consumer for the GitLab issuer. Classic FIC checks issuer + subject + audience by EXACT string comparison -- 'Wildcard characters aren't supported in any federated identity credential property value' -- and a subject mismatch fails the exchange with NO diagnostic ('You won't get an error, the exchange fails without error'). GitLab's default sub is path- and ref-scoped (project_path:{group}/{project}:ref_type:{branch|tag}:ref:{short_ref}); the immutable project_id/namespace_id are SEPARATE claims a classic FIC cannot reference. Three consequences pinned here: (1) exact match admits exactly one ref, so branch/tag/environment coverage multiplies FICs against Azure's hard cap ('A maximum of 20 federated identity credentials can be added to an application or user-assigned managed identity'), pushing admins toward broad subjects or the preview flexible-FIC expression language; (2) the AWS-StringLike habit of writing project_path:group/* is a literal that matches nothing (opposite of AWS, and silent); (3) because the default sub carries only the mutable path, a party who reclaims a deleted/renamed path mints a byte-identical subject the FIC cannot distinguish from the original -- mitigated platform-side by GitLab's June 2026 path-burn, but the FIC pins nothing immutable. The project_id-led sub is the policy-side fix.

vectors.description: Default name-based GitLab sub pinned to one branch of one project, matched by an exact classic FIC subject. The tight baseline -- but on GitLab's mutable path, not an immutable id.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: Scope is tight (one branch of one project), but the sub is the mutable project_path with no project_id anywhere in the FIC: a rename or transfer silently breaks it (fail-closed), and its safety against path reclamation rests entirely on GitLab's platform-side path-burn, since classic FIC cannot reference the immutable project_id claim. The project_id-led sub (see gitlab-az-fic-immutable-projectid-exact-match) is the durable pin.

vectors.description: Rename-proof GitLab sub after configuring ci_id_token_sub_claim_components so the sub leads with project_id, matched by an exact FIC. No mutable path anywhere in the credential.
vectors.subject: project_id:20:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: The sub leads with the immutable project_id -- which GitLab documents as 'globally unique and remains the same for the lifetime of the project' -- with no project_path anywhere in the credential, so an exact FIC match survives rename, transfer, and path-reuse while pinning one branch. Requires configuring ci_id_token_sub_claim_components so the sub leads with project_id: opt-in per project, not the default.

vectors.description: The wildcard trap in GitLab form, opposite to AWS and silent. An admin used to an AWS StringLike condition (where project_path:mygroup/* admits the whole group) writes the same string as a FIC subject. Classic FIC supports no wildcards in any property value, so '*' is a literal character: this subject matches NO real token and the credential silently authenticates nothing. Fails closed, but a correctness and portability trap that surfaces no error until the exchange fails.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

vectors.description: GitLab encodes ref_type (branch|tag) as its own sub segment, so a branch named v1.0 and a tag named v1.0 mint DIFFERENT subjects. A FIC pinned to the branch form here does not match the incoming TAG token, and the exchange fails silently. Under exact match this fails closed, but it is a GitLab-specific footgun absent from GitHub (whose sub fuses ref_type and ref into one refs/heads|refs/tags segment): the same short ref name is two distinct identities.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:tag:ref:v1.0

vectors.description: A different branch of the same project does not match an exact FIC subject -- the mechanical driver of FIC sprawl. Every branch and tag needs its own credential, against Azure's hard cap of 20 FICs per application or user-assigned managed identity. That ceiling is what pushes GitLab admins toward over-broad subjects or the preview flexible-FIC expression language, which trades classic FIC's exactness for a restricted match syntax.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:feature-x

vectors.description: The reclaimed-path token evaluated against the victim's stale FIC. The claims model a DIFFERENT project (project_id 999) now occupying mygroup/myproject and minting a byte-identical path-based sub; classic FIC matches only the string subject and has no way to reference project_id, so the squatter's token satisfies the credential. Mitigated upstream, not by the FIC: GitLab blocks new CI ID tokens for reclaimed project paths -- AWS's WIF note dates this platform-side change June 1, 2026, and GitLab commit f7335ef7e implements it (covering delete/rename/transfer; original project exempt; path-based subs only; non-retroactive; undocumented in GitLab's user docs). The GitLab path-reuse analogue of the GitHub-rename FIC break, and the Azure sibling of gitlab-aws-path-reuse-no-projectid.
vectors.subject: project_path:mygroup/myproject:ref_type:branch:ref:main

judgment.reason: Classic FIC pins the mutable project_path and cannot see the immutable project_id (999 here, not the original project's id), so whoever reclaims the vacated path assumes the federated identity. GitLab's platform-side path-burn closes the common reclaim route going forward, but it is non-retroactive, absent on self-managed instances predating the change, and invisible to the credential -- the FIC itself pins nothing immutable. The policy-side fix is the project_id-led sub (see gitlab-az-fic-immutable-projectid-exact-match); classic FIC has no condition-key layer to add a separate project_id check the way an AWS trust policy can.
